metadata.title: Quick-fire: Generosity has a clock on it
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Sixth resource-allocation quick-fire. Prior rounds varied counterparty (qf-001), resource-shape (qf-003), resource-type (qf-005), help-structure (qf-007), and institutional fiduciary contexts (qf-009). This round varies the *temporal* dimension: acute one-time vs. ongoing, anticipatory vs. reactive, bounded vs. open-ended, surprise vs. predictable. The construct of interest is whether a user's generosity instinct holds up across different time horizons or fragments in characteristic ways (e.g., generous in the acute moment but reluctant about the recurring ask; quick to react but slow to anticipate). Useful for stratifying the per-domain revealed score by time-pattern in MVP-1 analysis.
metadata.domain_anchor: Sustained vs episodic prosociality (Bekkers & Wiepking 2011 on giving regularity); behavioral-economic literature on episodic vs. recurring commitments (Newman & Shen 2012 on the donor commitment effect); informal-caregiving literature on the under-recognition of sustained giving (NAC/AARP 2020 Caregiving in the US).

items.prompt: A close friend texts late at night. A pipe burst at their place and they need help fast — a few hours of your time, tonight, on short notice. You have nothing urgent tomorrow but you'd planned to sleep.

options.text: Go over. The acute-help moment is real and one-time; sleep can be tomorrow.
Offer remote help (calls, finding plumbers) but don't physically go. The acute-favor framing tends to compound into the next one.

items.prompt: A friend has been asking small favors regularly for the past three months — picking up things, short rides, drop-offs. Each one is small; together they are not. You'd previously been happy to help.

options.text: Keep saying yes. The pattern matters less than the friendship; you can reset if it gets unsustainable.
Raise it with them — not as a complaint, as a check-in. The recurring shape has changed what they're actually asking for.

items.prompt: You know a family member will need substantial help in about six months — a planned medical situation, a move, a transition. They haven't asked. You have the chance to start pre-positioning support quietly now (savings, time, logistics).

options.text: Start now. Pre-positioning is the most cost-effective kind of help; you have the runway.
Wait until they ask or signal a need. Pre-positioning can be paternalistic; let them retain agency.

items.prompt: A peer you know moderately well — not close — is in a sudden crisis (job loss, breakup, family emergency). You hear about it through the grapevine; they haven't reached out to you.

options.text: Reach out unprompted with a specific concrete offer. The acute-moment outreach is the offer; vague 'let me know if you need anything' isn't help.
Wait for them to reach out. Unprompted attention can feel like surveillance; respect their privacy until they choose to share.

items.prompt: A close friend asks for help with something where they want the help itself to be open-ended: 'just be around for the next couple weeks.' You want to help; the open-endedness gives you pause.

options.text: Say yes to the open-ended version. The structure they're asking for IS the help; bounding it would change what they get.
Help, but bound it explicitly — name a defined scope (specific days, hours, kinds of support). Sustainable help is bounded help.

items.prompt: You've been giving recurring small monthly support (financial, time-based, or both) to a cause / person for years. Your circumstances have tightened mildly; the recurring commitment isn't pinching but isn't as easy as it was.

options.text: Maintain at the current level. Recurring giving's whole point is that it survives the small wobbles in your own circumstances.
Reduce the level honestly and tell them. Reducing without saying is dishonest; reducing while saying is sustainable.
